sub_tasks.requirements: Method and code fidelity to the paper

The All-CNN-C model is implemented exactly as in Table 2 (Section 3.1): every max-pooling layer of base model C is replaced by a 3x3 convolution with stride 2 whose number of output channels equals the number of input channels, and the network consists solely of convolutional layers with ReLU nonlinearities followed by a 1-by-1 convolution producing 10 outputs, spatial averaging over all positions, and a softmax (Section 2).
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: The three base models A, B, and C from Table 1 (Section 3.1) are implemented: model B as the Network-in-Network variant with one 1-by-1 convolution after each normal convolution layer, and model C with all 5x5 convolutions replaced by 3x3 convolutions, each topped by the 1-by-1 convolution + average + softmax head.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: The Strided-CNN-C variant (Table 2, Section 3.1) is implemented by removing max-pooling and increasing the stride of the convolution layer preceding each removed pooling layer by 1, so the next layer covers the same spatial region of the input as before.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: The ConvPool-CNN-C variant (Table 2, Section 3.1) is implemented by placing a dense convolution layer, with the same kernel size as the respective pooling layer, before each max-pooling layer, as the control for increased parameter count.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: Guided backpropagation is implemented as defined in Section 4 / Figure 1: when backpropagating through a ReLU, values are masked to zero whenever either the top (incoming) gradient or the bottom (forward) input is negative, combining the 'deconvnet' mask and the standard backpropagation mask, and it runs without max-pooling switches.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: Data and preprocessing fidelity
CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 (Krizhevsky & Hinton, 2009) images are whitened and contrast normalized following Goodfellow et al. (2013), as stated in the experimental setup of Section 3.2.

The data-augmentation runs use exactly the augmentations of Section 3.2: horizontally flipped copies of all images plus random translations with a maximum translation of 5 pixels in each dimension, and nothing beyond these.

The CIFAR-100 experiment reuses the identical All-CNN-C model and identical data pipeline and hyperparameters (learning rate and its schedule) used for CIFAR-10, with no per-dataset retuning, as specified in Section 3.2.
Experiment execution and reproducibility

Training logs/configs show all CIFAR networks trained with stochastic gradient descent with fixed momentum 0.9 for a total of 350 epochs, with the learning rate multiplied by 0.1 after epochs 200, 250, and 300 (schedule S=[200,250,300], Section 3.2).
Run artifacts show the learning rate γ was searched per model over exactly the fixed set {0.25, 0.1, 0.05, 0.01}, with results reported for the best γ per method, as described in Section 3.2.

Configs/code apply dropout with probability 20% on the input image and 50% after each pooling layer (or after the layer replacing pooling), plus weight decay λ=0.001 on all CIFAR models (Section 3.2).

The ImageNet run trains the upscaled All-CNN-B with 12 convolutional layers (conv1–conv12, Table 6 in the Appendix) for 450,000 iterations with batch size 64, initial learning rate γ=0.01 divided by 10 after every 200,000 iterations, and weight decay λ=0.0005 in all layers (Section 3).
Evaluation protocol and metric correctness

CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 results are reported as test error (%) on the standard held-out test sets, separately for the no-augmentation and augmentation settings, in the format of Tables 3 and 4 (Section 3.2).
ILSVRC-2012 performance is measured as Top-1 validation error evaluated only on the center 224x224 patch of each validation image, matching the protocol stated for the 41.2% result in Section 3.

Class probabilities at evaluation are produced by the paper's fully convolutional inference path (Section 2): the topmost 1-by-1 convolution yields 10 (or class-count) outputs per spatial position, which are averaged over all positions before the softmax — no fully connected classification head is used.
Result match versus the paper's reported targets
Reproduced base model A achieves approximately 12.5% test error on CIFAR-10 without data augmentation, matching the result reported in Section 3.2 / Table 3 (within a small tolerance, e.g. ±1%).
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: The reproduced metrics reproduce the qualitative ordering of Table 3 (Section 3.2): Strided-CNN-C performs worse than base model C, while All-CNN-C matches or improves on base model C, and ConvPool-CNN-C performs about on par with All-CNN-C.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced All-CNN-C test error on CIFAR-10 (with and/or without augmentation) is at or below the corresponding base-model-C error, reproducing the paper's central claim (Section 3.2, Table 4) that the all-convolutional network performs best among the considered models.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: The reproduced ImageNet All-CNN-B achieves a Top-1 validation error close to 41.2% on the ILSVRC-2012 center 224x224 evaluation, with a model containing fewer than 10 million parameters, as reported in Section 3 (compared against the 40.7% of Krizhevsky et al. 2012).
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: Artifact completeness and provenance

The repository contains runnable source code and configs for every evaluated variant — base models A/B/C, Strided-CNN-A/B/C, ConvPool-CNN-A/B/C, All-CNN-A/B/C (Tables 1–2, Section 3.1) — with the Section 3.2 hyperparameters (momentum 0.9, dropout 20%/50%, weight decay 0.001, schedule [200,250,300]) visible in the configs.

Per-run training logs and a machine-readable metrics file (e.g. metrics.json) record the final test error for each model variant on CIFAR-10 (and CIFAR-100 for All-CNN-C), traceable to the specific config/learning rate γ that produced each number (Section 3.2, Tables 3–4).

Saved visualization artifacts exist for the guided-backpropagation method of Section 4: reconstructed feature visualizations for lower layers (conv1–conv3, Figure 2) and/or higher layers (e.g. conv6/conv9, Figure 3), generated from the trained network.
